title: PyTorch on Apple MPS
description: Master GPU acceleration on Apple Silicon with Metal Performance Shaders. Learn device setup, tensor dtypes, and the critical fp64 trap that trips up newcomers.

sections.title: Why GPU Acceleration
sections.content:
### Why GPU Acceleration Matters Modern machine learning relies on **massive matrix multiplication operations**. A single transformer attention layer performs operations like `(batch, seq_len, hidden_dim) @ (hidden_dim, hidden_dim)` — potentially billions of floating-point operations per forward pass. CPUs are fantastic at sequential logic and branching. GPUs excel at the same operation applied to thousands of elements in parallel. GPU hardware dedicates transistors to arithmetic units rather than instruction caches, making them dramatically faster for bulk linear algebra. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["CPU (sequential logic)"] CPU_C["A few cores
~10s of threads
Wide ALUs, big caches"] end subgraph CUDA["NVIDIA GPU (CUDA)"] VRAM["Discrete VRAM (~80 GB)"] SM["1000s of cores"] VRAM -- "PCIe transfer
(explicit .cuda())" --> SM end subgraph MPS["Apple Silicon (MPS)"] UM["Unified memory
(CPU + GPU share RAM)"] GPUC["GPU cores +
Neural Engine"] UM -- "implicit, zero-copy" --> GPUC end Code["torch.tensor(...)"] --> CPU_C Code --> VRAM Code --> UM ``` For matmul-heavy workloads like training a transformer, well-utilized GPUs are typically **50–100x faster than a CPU**, and high-end datacenter GPUs can push that gap further. The exact speedup depends heavily on memory layout, batch size, and how saturated the arithmetic units actually are — never trust a single peak-FLOPS number on a spec sheet. ### What is MPS? **Metal Performance Shaders (MPS)** is Apple's GPU compute framework, analogous to NVIDIA's CUDA on their GPUs. PyTorch's MPS backend dispatches tensor ops to the Apple Silicon GPU cores via Metal. It leverages the unified-memory architecture and the GPU's wide SIMD units, typically achieving a meaningful fraction of high-end NVIDIA GPU performance for typical workloads while consuming far less power. ```python import torch # Check if MPS is available on this Apple Silicon Mac print(torch.backends.mps.is_available()) # True on M1/M2/M3+, False on Intel/older Macs # Move a tensor to MPS device device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu") x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device) print(x.device) # device(type='mps') ``` Note: PyTorch's MPS backend runs on the **GPU cores**, not on the Apple Neural Engine (ANE). The ANE is a separate accelerator only reachable through Core ML. If you want to target the ANE you have to convert to Core ML; if you want maximum native Apple-Silicon performance from Python directly, see Apple's **MLX** framework. We stick with PyTorch in this course because it is portable across CUDA, MPS, and CPU.

sections.title: MPS vs CUDA
sections.content:
### Key Differences from CUDA

MPS is powerful but has gotchas compared to CUDA:

| Feature | CUDA | MPS |
|---------|------|-----|
| **Float64 (fp64)** | Fully supported | Not supported; will error |
| **Device transfer syntax** | Both should use `.to(device)`. `.cuda()` exists historically but is no longer recommended. | Same — use `.to(device)`. There is no `.mps()` shorthand by design. |
| **Unified memory** | Optional, explicit | Default; no explicit sync needed |
| **Debugging** | cuda-gdb, Nsight, profilers | Limited tooling |
| **Vendor** | NVIDIA only | Apple Silicon only |
| **Power efficiency** | ~300W for A100 | ~10–30W typical for M-series |
| **Memory bandwidth** | High-end datacenter GPUs: 2–3+ TB/s (A100, H100) | Apple Silicon: ~100 GB/s base · ~400 GB/s Max · ~800 GB/s Ultra |

### Why These Differences Exist

CUDA assumes discrete GPUs with separate memory (VRAM), requiring explicit CPU↔GPU transfers. Apple Silicon has **unified memory**: the same physical RAM serves CPU and GPU, so transfers are implicit and zero-copy. This design choice trades explicit control for developer convenience, and has very different performance characteristics: discrete GPUs win on raw bandwidth, unified memory wins on transfer-free interop.

### The fp64 Gotcha (The #1 Source of Runtime Errors)

Creating an fp64 (double-precision float) tensor on MPS will crash with a cryptic RuntimeError:

```python
# Incorrect: this raises RuntimeError on MPS
x = torch.randn(100, dtype=torch.float64, device=torch.device("mps"))
# RuntimeError: float64 is not supported on MPS

# Correct: always use float32 (default) on MPS
x = torch.randn(100, dtype=torch.float32, device=torch.device("mps"))
# or simply (float32 is default):
x = torch.randn(100, device=torch.device("mps"))
```

**Why does this happen?** The Apple Silicon GPU lacks 64-bit floating-point hardware units; only the CPU has hardware fp64. The MPS backend therefore refuses fp64 rather than silently downcasting and corrupting your numerics.

This often happens accidentally when:
- A function defaults to float64 (some scientific libs do this)
- You load a model trained on CUDA (which might have parameters stored as float64)
- A library internally promotes to float64 for numerical stability
- You call `.double()` on a model without thinking

sections.title: Device-Agnostic Patterns
sections.content:
### Device-Agnostic Code Pattern

**Best practice**: write code that works on any device without hardcoding `"mps"` or `"cuda"`.

```python
# Pattern 1: Try MPS, fall back to CPU
device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu")
x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device)
model = MyModel().to(device)  # .to() works on any device

# Pattern 2: Full hierarchy (CUDA > MPS > CPU)
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    device = "cuda"
elif torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device = "mps"
else:
    device = "cpu"
device = torch.device(device)
```

### MPS Fallback: When Operations Aren't Supported

Some operations don't yet have an MPS implementation. PyTorch can fall back to CPU automatically:

```bash
PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 python train.py
```

This environment variable tells PyTorch: "if an op fails on MPS, silently run it on CPU instead." Useful during development; avoid in production because it can introduce silent CPU↔GPU round-trips that wreck performance. Better is to identify the offending op explicitly:

```python
if device.type == "mps":
    result = some_unsupported_op(x.cpu()).to(device)
else:
    result = some_unsupported_op(x)
```

### `torch.compile` on MPS

`torch.compile` (the TorchInductor JIT) is supported on MPS but coverage is partial. Many ops will compile and run faster; others will fall back to eager mode silently. Treat compile speedups on MPS as a bonus, not a guarantee — benchmark the compiled vs. uncompiled version of your specific model before relying on it.

### Reproducibility and Seeding

For deterministic experiments, seed every RNG you use, including the MPS device and any non-PyTorch libraries:

```python
import random, numpy as np, torch

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)        # seeds CPU and (where applicable) all CUDA devices
torch.mps.manual_seed(SEED)    # MPS-specific RNG
# torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)  # optional, may slow training
```

Bit-exact reproducibility on GPU is **not free**: many fast kernels use non-deterministic reductions. Setting `use_deterministic_algorithms(True)` forces deterministic implementations where available and raises an error elsewhere — useful for debugging, costly for throughput.

### Unified Memory: Why It Matters

Apple Silicon has **unified memory**: CPU and GPU access the same RAM without explicit copying. CUDA requires you to manage PCIe transfers (`.to("cuda")`, `.to("cpu")`); MPS handles this transparently, reducing boilerplate and a whole class of "tensor on wrong device" bugs.

The tradeoff is bandwidth: high-end NVIDIA datacenter GPUs deliver 2–3+ TB/s of memory bandwidth; Apple Silicon ranges from roughly 100 GB/s on base M-series chips to ~800 GB/s on Ultra-tier parts. That is why memory-bound operations on MPS can be slower than on top-end NVIDIA hardware even when peak FLOPS look comparable.

### Memory Introspection on MPS

When training crashes with OOM, you need to know what your process is actually using. The `torch.mps` namespace exposes a small but essential API:

```python
import torch

# Bytes currently allocated to live tensors
print(torch.mps.current_allocated_memory())
# Bytes the driver has actually reserved (>= current_allocated, includes cached blocks)
print(torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory())

# Free the cached (unused) blocks back to the system; does NOT free live tensors
torch.mps.empty_cache()
```

`current_allocated_memory()` is your live working set; `driver_allocated_memory()` includes the allocator's free list. The gap between them is fragmentation. `empty_cache()` is useful when interleaving heavy training with other GPU users on the same machine — but it cannot rescue a process that genuinely needs more memory than it has.

sections.title: MPS Pitfalls & Synchronization
sections.content:
### Pitfall 1: Mixing dtypes Between Layers
```python
# Incorrect: creates dtype mismatch
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float().to("mps")
model.weight = nn.Parameter(model.weight.double())  # weight now float64
x = torch.randn(1, 10, dtype=torch.float32, device="mps")
y = model(x)  # Will fail: can't multiply float32 input with float64 weight

# Correct: keep everything float32
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float().to("mps")
x = torch.randn(1, 10, dtype=torch.float32, device="mps")
y = model(x)  # Works; both are float32
```

### Pitfall 2: Forgetting to Move Data to Device
```python
# Incorrect: model on MPS, data on CPU
device = torch.device("mps")
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).to(device)
x = torch.randn(1, 10)  # On CPU by default
y = model(x)  # RuntimeError: expected all tensors on the same device

# Correct: move both model and data
device = torch.device("mps")
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).to(device)
x = torch.randn(1, 10).to(device)  # Move data too
y = model(x)  # Works
```

### Pitfall 3: Not Synchronizing Before Timing
```python
# Incorrect: GPU operations are async; the timer fires before the GPU finishes
import time
start = time.perf_counter()
x = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
y = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
z = x @ y  # GPU operation queued, not finished yet
end = time.perf_counter()
print(f"Time: {end - start}")  # Reports launch time, not actual compute time

# Correct: synchronize before stopping the clock
import time
start = time.perf_counter()
x = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
y = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
z = x @ y
torch.mps.synchronize()  # Wait for GPU to finish
end = time.perf_counter()
print(f"Time: {end - start}")
```

GPU launches are queued asynchronously: the Python-side call returns as soon as the kernel is enqueued, not when it finishes. Without `torch.mps.synchronize()` (or `torch.cuda.synchronize()` on CUDA) your timer measures launch latency, not compute time, and can be off by orders of magnitude.
sections.code:
import torch
import time

# 1. Check MPS availability
print(f"MPS available: {torch.backends.mps.is_available()}")

# 2. Create device-agnostic setup
device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

# 3. Create and move tensors
x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device)
print(f"Tensor device: {x.device}, dtype: {x.dtype}")

# 4. Device transfer with .to()
model = torch.nn.Linear(100, 10)
model = model.to(device)
print(f"Model moved to: {next(model.parameters()).device}")

# 5. Avoid fp64 on MPS (demonstrate error gracefully)
try:
    bad_tensor = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float64, device=device)
except (RuntimeError, TypeError) as e:
    print(f"Error (expected on MPS): {str(e)[:80]}...")

# 6. Always use float32 on MPS
good_tensor = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
print(f"Created fp32 tensor: {good_tensor.shape}, dtype: {good_tensor.dtype}")

# 7. Memory introspection (MPS-only)
if device.type == "mps":
    print(f"Allocated: {torch.mps.current_allocated_memory() / 1e6:.2f} MB")
    print(f"Driver-reserved: {torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory() / 1e6:.2f} MB")

# 8. Benchmark: simple matmul
x = torch.randn(2000, 2000, device=device)
y = torch.randn(2000, 2000, device=device)
if device.type == "mps":
    torch.mps.synchronize()
start = time.perf_counter()
z = x @ y
if device.type == "mps":
    torch.mps.synchronize()
end = time.perf_counter()
print(f"Matmul (2000x2000) took {(end-start)*1000:.2f}ms")

sections.title: What is a Tensor?
sections.content:
### Tensors: the unit of every computation

A **tensor** is a multi-dimensional array of numbers. In PyTorch:

```python
x = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3])  # 1-D: shape (3,)
y = torch.randn(2, 3, 4)     # 3-D: shape (2, 3, 4)
z = torch.ones(5)             # 1-D: shape (5,)
```

Each tensor has four critical properties:

1. **Shape** (dimensions): `x.shape` — used for broadcasting and linear-algebra checks.
2. **Dtype** (data type): `x.dtype` — determines precision and speed.
3. **Device**: `x.device` — CPU, MPS, CUDA, etc.
4. **Requires_grad**: `x.requires_grad` — whether to track gradients for training (covered in Module 2).

```python
x = torch.randn(3, 4, 5)
print(f"Shape: {x.shape}")           # torch.Size([3, 4, 5])
print(f"Dtype: {x.dtype}")           # torch.float32
print(f"Device: {x.device}")         # cpu
print(f"Requires grad: {x.requires_grad}")  # False (by default)
```

### Why This Matters

Tensors are the **fundamental unit** in deep learning. Everything — inputs, weights, activations, gradients — is a tensor. Understanding their properties is critical because:

1. **Shape mismatches crash your model.** Matrix multiplication requires compatible dimensions; checking and manipulating shapes prevents silent bugs.
2. **Dtype determines accuracy and speed.** float32 is many times faster than float64 on most GPUs, but float16 can underflow tiny gradients.
3. **Device placement determines where computation happens.** The same code can run 100x slower if data sits on the wrong device.
4. **`requires_grad` turns on backpropagation.** Without it, `.backward()` won't compute gradients.

sections.title: Tensor Operations: Creation, Indexing, Broadcasting
sections.content:
### Creating Tensors

The most common factory functions every PyTorch user should know:

```python
import torch

torch.zeros(3, 4)               # all zeros, shape (3, 4)
torch.ones(2, 3)                # all ones
torch.full((2, 3), 7.0)          # filled with a constant
torch.eye(4)                    # identity matrix
torch.arange(0, 10, 2)           # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]
torch.linspace(0, 1, 5)          # [0.00, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00]
torch.randn(2, 3)               # standard normal
torch.rand(2, 3)                # uniform [0, 1)
torch.empty(2, 3)               # uninitialized (faster, contains garbage)
torch.zeros_like(x)             # match shape/dtype/device of x
```

Prefer `*_like` and `dtype=`/`device=` arguments at creation time — it is far cheaper to allocate on the right device than to allocate on CPU and `.to(device)` afterwards.

### Indexing and Advanced Selection

PyTorch supports NumPy-style basic slicing plus several powerful selection patterns:

```python
x = torch.arange(24).reshape(4, 6)

x[0]          # row 0
x[:, 1]       # column 1
x[1:3, 2:5]   # 2x3 block

# Boolean mask
mask = x > 10
x[mask]                    # 1-D tensor of all elements > 10

# Integer index ("fancy" indexing)
rows = torch.tensor([0, 2, 3])
x[rows]                    # rows 0, 2, 3 (shape 3x6)

# torch.gather: pick one element per row along a dim
idx = torch.tensor([[1], [3], [0], [5]])  # one column index per row
x.gather(dim=1, index=idx)                # shape (4, 1)

# torch.scatter: write values into positions
out = torch.zeros(4, 6, dtype=torch.long)
out.scatter_(dim=1, index=idx, src=torch.ones_like(idx))
```

`gather`/`scatter` are the workhorses behind one-hot encoding, top-k selection, and sparse routing layers (e.g. Mixture-of-Experts).

### Broadcasting

Broadcasting lets PyTorch operate on tensors of different shapes without explicit copies. The rule, applied right-to-left over the dims:

1. Pad the shorter shape on the left with 1s until ranks match.
2. Two dims are compatible if they are equal or one of them is 1.
3. The output shape takes the larger of the two on each dim.

```python
a = torch.randn(4, 1, 6)   # shape (4, 1, 6)
b = torch.randn(   3, 6)   # shape (3, 6) -> treated as (1, 3, 6)
(a + b).shape              # (4, 3, 6)
```

A frequent bug: silently broadcasting where you meant to error. If you compute `pred - target` and one is `(N,)` while the other is `(N, 1)`, you get an `(N, N)` outer-difference instead of an `(N,)` residual. Always print shapes when results look weird.

### `view`, `reshape`, `permute`, `transpose`, `contiguous`

These four operations cause more attention-implementation bugs than anything else.

- `view(shape)` — returns a new view with the requested shape; **requires the tensor to be contiguous** in memory. Fast (no copy).
- `reshape(shape)` — same intent, but copies if needed. Always works.
- `transpose(dim0, dim1)` — swap two dims. Returns a non-contiguous view.
- `permute(*dims)` — arbitrary reordering of dims. Also non-contiguous.
- `.contiguous()` — make a fresh, contiguous copy. Required before `view` after a `transpose`/`permute`.

```python
x = torch.randn(2, 3, 4)
y = x.transpose(0, 1)              # shape (3, 2, 4), NOT contiguous
# y.view(3, 8)                    # RuntimeError: view requires contiguous
y = y.contiguous().view(3, 8)     # OK
# Or just:
y = x.transpose(0, 1).reshape(3, 8)
```

### Einstein Summation: `einsum`

`einsum` is the most readable way to express a contraction. The string names each axis; repeated names are summed, named axes appearing in the output are kept:

```python
# Batched matmul: A @ B for each batch
A = torch.randn(8, 4, 5)   # (batch, m, k)
B = torch.randn(8, 5, 6)   # (batch, k, n)
C = torch.einsum('bmk,bkn->bmn', A, B)   # (batch, m, n)

# Attention scores: (B, H, Q, D) x (B, H, K, D) -> (B, H, Q, K)
Q = torch.randn(2, 4, 10, 8)
K = torch.randn(2, 4, 12, 8)
scores = torch.einsum('bhqd,bhkd->bhqk', Q, K)
```

The equivalent without `einsum` is `torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1))` — same result, more boilerplate and easier to misalign axes. For attention, scaling, masking, and complex contractions, `einsum` is usually clearer.

sections.title: Floating-Point Dtypes
sections.content:
### Floating-Point Dtypes: The Precision-Speed Tradeoff

All floating-point formats follow IEEE 754: a sign bit, exponent bits, and mantissa bits.

| Dtype | Bits | Exponent | Mantissa | Use Case | Support |
|-------|------|----------|----------|----------|---------|
| **float32** | 32 | 8 | 23 | Default; training, inference | All |
| **float16** (half) | 16 | 5 | 10 | Speed, reduced memory | GPU-only |
| **bfloat16** | 16 | 8 | 7 | Training without convergence loss | Modern GPUs, MPS |
| **float64** | 64 | 11 | 52 | Scientific computing | CPU, CUDA (not MPS) |

```cudadiagram
FloatFormatBits
```

### Understanding the Tradeoff

**Exponent bits** determine the **range** of representable numbers. More exponent bits → wider range.
**Mantissa bits** determine the **precision** (how close numbers can be together). More mantissa bits → finer resolution.

- **float32**: 8 exponent (range ~10^-38 to 10^38), 23 stored mantissa bits → ~7 decimal digits of precision.
- **float16**: 5 exponent (normal range ~6e-5 to 65504), 10 stored mantissa bits → ~3 decimal digits.
- **bfloat16**: 8 exponent (same range as fp32), 7 stored mantissa bits + 1 implicit = 8 bits of significand → ~3 decimal digits.
- **float64**: 11 exponent (range ~10^-308 to 10^308), 52 stored mantissa bits → ~15–17 decimal digits.

On consumer GPUs, fp64 throughput is often only 1/32 or 1/64 of fp32; on datacenter chips like A100 it is 1/2. Concretely, switching from fp64 to fp32 typically buys you anywhere from **2x to 32x** throughput depending on hardware — and on MPS it is the difference between "works" and "crashes".

sections.title: bfloat16 vs float16
sections.content:
### Why bfloat16 Beats float16 for Training

During neural-network training, gradients become very small. **float16** has only 5 exponent bits, so numbers below roughly 6×10⁻⁵ leave the normal range and start losing precision; below ~6×10⁻⁸ they vanish to zero entirely (denormal underflow). Once gradients underflow, weights stop updating and training stalls.

```python
import torch

# float16 cannot represent 1e-5 precisely; the closest representable value
# is just above ~9.5e-6, and you lose multiple decimal digits of accuracy.
x_fp16 = torch.tensor(1e-5, dtype=torch.float16)
print(x_fp16.item())   # something near 1e-5 but with visible quantization

# bfloat16 has the same 8 exponent bits as float32, so 1e-5 is well within range
x_bf16 = torch.tensor(1e-5, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
print(x_bf16.item())   # ~1.0e-5 (less mantissa precision, but range is fine)
```

When you run this yourself, copy the actual printed values rather than trusting any tutorial's hand-typed example — IEEE rounding rules don't care about our intuitions.

**bfloat16** keeps the full 8 exponent bits (like float32) but sacrifices mantissa precision instead. This is **perfect for training**: you need wide range, and neural nets are remarkably resilient to small precision losses in the bottom mantissa bits.

- **float32**: safe default; use when training is slow.
- **bfloat16**: best for training large models; combine with `torch.autocast()` for automatic mixed precision.
- **float16**: inference only without loss scaling — gradient underflow is severe. With `GradScaler` and care, fp16 training is viable on Volta/Turing-era CUDA hardware, but bf16 is the modern default where supported.
- **float64**: never on MPS; on CPU/CUDA only when doing scientific computing where extreme precision matters.

### Mixed Precision Training: float32 + bfloat16

Modern training uses **automatic mixed precision (AMP)**: compute most operations in bfloat16 (fast, low memory) but keep the loss and gradient master copy in float32 (accurate updates).

```python
model = MyModel().to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters())

for x, y in dataloader:
    x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)

    # Forward pass with automatic mixed precision (AMP)
    with torch.autocast(device_type=device.type, dtype=torch.bfloat16):
        logits = model(x)              # operations cast to bfloat16
        loss = criterion(logits, y)    # loss returned in float32

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
```

This is typically **10–30% faster** than pure float32 training on modern GPUs/MPS, with no measurable accuracy loss for most architectures.

sections.title: The fp64 Trap & Solutions
sections.content:
### The fp64 Trap (Again, Because It Bites Everyone) This is **THE** gotcha on MPS. A very common mistake: ```python import torch.nn as nn model = nn.Linear(10, 5) # default dtype is float32 model = model.to("mps") # But if you call .double() (-> float64), it breaks on MPS: model = model.double() # RuntimeError: float64 is not supported on MPS # Or when loading a model from CUDA (which might have float64 weights): checkpoint = torch.load("model.pt") # might have float64 params model.load_state_dict(checkpoint) # RuntimeError on MPS # Some pipelines silently promote intermediates to float64 for stability loss = torch.nn.functional.l1_loss(pred, target) ``` ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Create / load tensor"] --> B{"dtype == float64?"} B -- "No (fp32/bf16/fp16)" --> C["Move to MPS"] C --> OK(["Works"]) B -- "Yes" --> D{"On MPS?"} D -- "No (CPU/CUDA)" --> OK D -- "Yes" --> E["RuntimeError:
float64 not supported on MPS"] E --> F["Fix: .float() / map_location='cpu' /
explicit dtype=torch.float32"] F --> C ``` ### Solutions **Solution 1**: explicitly specify float32 during model creation: ```python model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float() # explicit float32 model = model.to("mps") ``` **Solution 2**: convert your entire model to a safe dtype after loading: ```python def safe_load_on_device(model, checkpoint_path, device): checkpoint = torch.load(checkpoint_path, map_location=device) model.load_state_dict(checkpoint) return model.float().to(device) # ensure float32 ``` **Solution 3**: use `map_location='cpu'` when loading, then move: ```python checkpoint = torch.load("model.pt", map_location="cpu") model.load_state_dict(checkpoint) model = model.float().to("mps") # convert and move ``` **Solution 4**: cast any stray fp64 parameters in-place before moving. Use `no_grad` and `copy_` rather than touching `.data` directly: ```python with torch.no_grad(): for param in model.parameters(): if param.dtype == torch.float64: param.copy_(param.float()) model = model.to("mps") ``` ### NumPy interop A related dtype trap: `numpy` defaults to `float64` for many constructors. `torch.from_numpy(np.ones(3))` gives you a float64 tensor — fine on CPU, broken on MPS. ```python import numpy as np arr = np.ones(3) # dtype float64 t = torch.from_numpy(arr) # float64 tensor! # t = t.to("mps") # would error # Safer t = torch.from_numpy(arr).float().to("mps") # Or: arr = arr.astype(np.float32) first # Going back to NumPy: the tensor must be on CPU t_cpu = t.detach().cpu() np_arr = t_cpu.numpy() # shares memory with t_cpu ``` `tensor.numpy()` requires a CPU tensor — on MPS or CUDA you must `.cpu()` first. The returned array shares memory with the CPU tensor (unless you `.clone()`), so mutating one mutates the other. ### Practical Dtype Selection Guide - **float32**: default. Safe, widely supported. Use unless you have a specific reason. - **bfloat16**: training large models (transformers). Combine with `torch.autocast()` for AMP. - **float16**: inference on mobile/edge, or training with `GradScaler` on older CUDA hardware. Validate convergence carefully. - **float64**: never on MPS. On CPU/CUDA only when doing genuine scientific computing. - **int8 (quantization)**: inference only; requires post-training or quantization-aware training.

sections.title: Dtype Pitfalls
sections.content:
### Pitfall 1: Creating Tensors Without Specifying Device
```python
# Incorrect: tensor created on CPU by default
device = "mps"
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).to(device)
x = torch.randn(1, 10)  # on CPU!
y = model(x)  # RuntimeError: expected all tensors on mps

# Correct: specify device at creation
x = torch.randn(1, 10, device=device)
```

### Pitfall 2: Assuming float32 Solves All Problems
```python
# Suboptimal: float32 works, but is slower and uses 2x the memory of bf16
model = MyModel().float().to("mps")

# Better for training: AMP with bfloat16
with torch.autocast(device_type="mps", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
    loss = criterion(model(x), y)
```

### Pitfall 3: Mixing Dtypes in Batch Operations
```python
# Incorrect: stacking float32 and float64
a = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float32)
b = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float64)
c = torch.stack([a, b])  # promotes to float64 silently — fine on CPU,
                          # crashes when you later move to MPS

# Correct: ensure consistent dtype
a = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float32)
b = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float32)
c = torch.stack([a, b])  # works
```
sections.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# 1. Tensor properties
x = torch.randn(2, 3, 4)
print(f"Shape: {x.shape}, Dtype: {x.dtype}, Device: {x.device}, Requires grad: {x.requires_grad}")

# 2. Create tensors with specific dtypes
device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu")
a = torch.randn(5, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
b = torch.randn(5, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device=device)
c = torch.randn(5, dtype=torch.float16, device=device)

print("\nDtype comparison:")
print(f"float32: {a.dtype}, bfloat16: {b.dtype}, float16: {c.dtype}")

# 3. Demonstrate float16 underflow with small numbers
x_fp16 = torch.tensor(1e-5, dtype=torch.float16)
x_bf16 = torch.tensor(1e-5, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
x_fp32 = torch.tensor(1e-5, dtype=torch.float32)
print("\nSmall value (1e-5) representation:")
print(f"float16: {x_fp16.item():.10e}")
print(f"bfloat16: {x_bf16.item():.10e}")
print(f"float32: {x_fp32.item():.10e}")

# 4. Create model and check dtype
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float().to(device)
print(f"\nModel on device: {next(model.parameters()).device}")
print(f"Model dtype: {next(model.parameters()).dtype}")

# 5. The fp64 trap (commented out to avoid crash on MPS)
print("\nfp64 trap: uncomment below to test")
# bad_tensor = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float64, device=device)
# RuntimeError on MPS: float64 is not supported

# 6. Safe dtype conversion pattern
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).to(device).float()
print(f"Safe model dtype: {next(model.parameters()).dtype}")

# 7. Mixed precision setup example (modern API)
if device.type in ("mps", "cuda"):
    autocast_dtype = torch.bfloat16  # bf16 works on both
    print(f"\nUsing autocast with {autocast_dtype}")
    if device.type == "cuda":
        scaler = torch.amp.GradScaler("cuda")  # only used with fp16
        print("GradScaler ready for fp16 training (cuda only)")

# 8. Tensor creation with explicit device
x = torch.randn(100, 50, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
print(f"\nCreated tensor: shape {x.shape}, device {x.device}, dtype {x.dtype}")

references.title: bfloat16: A New Brain Floating Point Format (Google Research)

quiz.question: How do you safely check if MPS is available on the current machine?
options: torch.mps.is_available()
torch.backends.mps.is_available()
torch.device("mps").is_available()
sys.platform == "darwin"
quiz.explanation: torch.backends.mps.is_available() is the official PyTorch API. The first option is incorrect (no .mps module at top level). The third doesn't exist. The fourth checks the OS but not MPS specifically (Intel Macs run darwin too).

quiz.question: What happens if you try to create a float64 tensor on MPS?
options: It automatically converts to float32
It silently uses CPU instead
It raises a RuntimeError
It creates the tensor but operations are slow
quiz.explanation: MPS hardware does not have float64 support. PyTorch will raise a RuntimeError. It does not auto-convert or silently fall back without PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 set.

quiz.question: Why is bfloat16 preferred over float16 for neural network training?
options: bfloat16 is faster on all hardware
float16 has insufficient exponent bits, causing gradient underflow; bfloat16 preserves the exponent range
bfloat16 uses less memory than float16
float16 is not supported by PyTorch
quiz.explanation: float16 has only 5 exponent bits (~6e-5 to 65504 normal range), so tiny gradients underflow to zero. bfloat16 keeps 8 exponent bits like float32, preserving range while sacrificing precision—perfect for training where small precision loss is acceptable but gradient range matters.

quiz.question: What is a key advantage of Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture?
options: Data is faster to access than on NVIDIA GPUs
You don't need to explicitly manage data transfers between CPU and GPU
It automatically converts fp64 to fp32
It provides better debugging tools than CUDA
quiz.explanation: Unified memory means CPU and GPU share the same address space, so you don't need explicit .to("cuda") transfers like on NVIDIA. The OS handles copying transparently. (Speed can be slower than NVIDIA on memory-bound ops, and debugging tooling is limited.)

quiz.question: When is it appropriate to use float16 on MPS?
options: Always; it's the fastest dtype on MPS
Never; float16 is not supported on MPS
For inference only, after careful validation that gradients don't underflow
For training large models to reduce memory usage
quiz.explanation: float16 has insufficient exponent bits for training (gradients underflow). It's suitable only for inference, where you don't compute gradients and the limited precision is acceptable. Validation is critical to ensure no accuracy loss.

quiz.question: What environment variable enables CPU fallback for unsupported MPS operations?
options: MPS_ENABLE_FALLBACK=1
PYTORCH_ENABLE_FALLBACK=1
PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1
MPS_USE_CPU=1
quiz.explanation: PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 tells PyTorch to fall back to CPU for unsupported operations. Useful for development but should be avoided in production due to silent performance degradation.

exercise.title: MPS Device Benchmark
exercise.description:
Create tensors on CPU and MPS, benchmark matrix multiplication at increasing sizes, and report the speedup factor.

**Requirements:**
- Create square matrices of sizes: 256, 512, 1024, 2048
- For each size, benchmark matmul on both CPU and MPS
- Use `torch.mps.synchronize()` before measuring MPS time (ensures all GPU ops complete)
- Report speedup as CPU_time / MPS_time for each size
- Handle the case where MPS is unavailable gracefully
- Use consistent number of iterations (5) for all sizes

**Hints:**
- Use `time.perf_counter()` for timing (more precise than `time.time()`)
- Warm up the GPU with one iteration before timing
- Run at least 5 iterations per size to reduce noise
- MPS synchronization is critical; without it, timers fire before GPU finishes
- Print results in a clear table format for easy comparison
exercise.starterCode:
import torch
import time

# Note: torch.device(None) raises TypeError, so build the device only when MPS is
# actually available and fall back to plain CPU otherwise.
if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device_mps = torch.device("mps")
else:
    device_mps = torch.device("cpu")
device_cpu = torch.device("cpu")

def benchmark_matmul(size, device, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark matrix multiplication on a given device."""
    # Create random matrices
    a = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)
    b = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)

    # Warm up (1 iteration)
    _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()

    # Timed iterations
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()
    end = time.perf_counter()

    return (end - start) / num_iters

# TODO: Test matmul at sizes [256, 512, 1024, 2048]
# TODO: Compute speedup (CPU time / MPS time) for each size
# TODO: Print results in a table with columns: Size, CPU (ms), MPS (ms), Speedup (x)
# TODO: Handle case where MPS is unavailable
exercise.solution:
import torch
import time

mps_available = torch.backends.mps.is_available()
device_mps = torch.device("mps") if mps_available else torch.device("cpu")
device_cpu = torch.device("cpu")

def benchmark_matmul(size, device, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark matrix multiplication on a given device."""
    a = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)
    b = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)

    # Warm up
    _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()

    # Timed iterations
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()
    end = time.perf_counter()

    return (end - start) / num_iters

# Benchmark at different sizes
sizes = [256, 512, 1024, 2048]
print("\nMatrix Multiplication Benchmark (avg time per iter)\n")
print(f"{'Size':<8} {'CPU (ms)':<12} {'MPS (ms)':<12} {'Speedup':<10}")
print("-" * 42)

if not mps_available:
    print("MPS not available on this device; running CPU baseline only.")
    for size in sizes:
        cpu_time = benchmark_matmul(size, device_cpu, num_iters=5) * 1000
        print(f"{size:<8} {cpu_time:<12.3f} {'n/a':<12} {'n/a':<10}")
else:
    for size in sizes:
        cpu_time = benchmark_matmul(size, device_cpu, num_iters=5) * 1000
        mps_time = benchmark_matmul(size, device_mps, num_iters=5) * 1000
        speedup = cpu_time / mps_time
        print(f"{size:<8} {cpu_time:<12.3f} {mps_time:<12.3f} {speedup:<10.2f}x")

print("\nSpeedup > 1.0 means MPS is faster. Speedup may vary based on hardware.")
